name: Southern Peninsula
history: The Southern Peninsula of Thailand, known as Isan's southern extension, developed as a distinct region through centuries of Malay, Islamic, and Thai cultural interchange. The area was historically influenced by the Srivijaya Empire and later became part of various Thai kingdoms, with the southern provinces gradually integrated into the modern Thai state. Muslim kingdoms and sultanates held significant power in the region until Thai consolidation in the 18th and 19th centuries. This complex history of competing empires left a rich cultural tapestry that distinguishes the south from central Thailand.
culturalNotes: The Southern Peninsula is Thailand's most culturally and religiously diverse region, with Islam being the dominant faith in many provinces and Muslim traditions deeply embedded in daily life, cuisine, and festivals. The region is renowned for its distinctive food culture, featuring spicy curries, fresh seafood, and unique dishes like satay and roti that reflect Malay influences. Traditional crafts, shadow puppet theater (Nang Talung), and vibrant markets showcase a blend of Thai, Malay, Chinese, and Indian cultural elements that create a unique southern identity.
bestTimeToVisit: December to March offers the driest weather for island exploration and diving.
languageNotes: While Thai is the official language, Southern Thai dialect and Malay are widely spoken, particularly in the southernmost provinces of Pattani, Yala, and Narathiwat. Useful phrases include 'Sawasdee krap' (hello) in Thai and 'Assalamualaikum' in Malay, which shows respect in Muslim-majority areas and is appreciated by local communities.

places.name: Surat Thani
places.nameIt: สุราษฎร์ธานี
places.description: The largest province in southern Thailand and gateway to the beautiful Koh Samui and Koh Tao islands. It serves as an important transportation hub with beaches, forests, and access to some of Thailand's best diving spots.

activities.detail: Explore Thailand's oldest rainforest with limestone cliffs, emerald lakes, and jungle wildlife just 90 minutes from the city.
Take a short ferry to Thailand's most famous island to enjoy white-sand beaches, water sports, and vibrant nightlife.
Visit this premier diving destination for world-class coral reefs and the chance to encounter manta rays and sea turtles.
Paddle through dramatic limestone karsts and stay in floating bungalows in Khao Sok's most scenic lake.
Wander the bustling local market to experience authentic Thai street food and traditional handicrafts.
Island-hop through 40 protected islands with pristine beaches, snorkeling, and kayaking opportunities.

activities.text: Khao Sam Roi Yot National Park
activities.detail: Discover limestone mountains, sea caves, hidden lagoons, and beaches near the Prachuap Khiri Khan border.

food.detail: A tangy southern Thai curry made with turmeric, shrimp paste, and tamarind that's lighter and sourer than northern curries.
Grilled meat skewers served with a spicy mango dipping sauce unique to the southern Surat Thani region.
A simple rice dish mixed with herbs, shrimp paste, and fresh vegetables traditionally eaten for breakfast in southern Thailand.

food.text: Fresh Seafood at the Night Market
food.detail: Grilled fish, squid, and shrimp from local waters served at street stalls in Surat Thani's evening market.
A peppery fish belly curry considered a local specialty and often served at family-run restaurants throughout the province.

culture.detail: Always greet people with a wai (palms together, slight bow) which is especially important when meeting elders or entering temples.
Take off your shoes when entering temples, homes, and some traditional establishments as a sign of respect.
Wear long pants or skirts and cover your shoulders when visiting Buddhist temples; avoid sleeveless tops and short shorts.
Locals greatly appreciate visitors who attempt polite Thai greetings like 'Sawasdee krap/ka' and 'Khop khun krap/ka' (thank you).

places.name: Koh Samui
places.description: Thailand's second-largest island, Koh Samui is known for luxury resorts, excellent beaches, and the famous Big Buddha statue. It offers a perfect blend of natural beauty, nightlife, and upscale amenities.

activities.detail: Visit the iconic 12-meter golden Buddha statue seated on a hilltop with panoramic island views and a functioning Buddhist temple below.
Take a boat tour through this protected archipelago of 42 limestone islands with emerald lagoons, hidden beaches, and excellent snorkeling opportunities.
Relax on Koh Samui's longest and most popular beach, lined with upscale resorts, water sports facilities, and beachfront restaurants and bars.
Experience the island's second-largest beach with a more relaxed vibe than Chaweng, featuring interesting rock formations and excellent sunset views.
Explore a hidden garden sanctuary in the island's interior filled with Buddha statues, stone sculptures, and tranquil water features nestled among lush vegetation.
Trek through jungle to reach this two-tiered waterfall where you can swim in the natural pools and enjoy the cool, refreshing mountain water.
Learn to prepare authentic Thai dishes from experienced instructors, including visits to local markets and hands-on preparation of curries, stir-fries, and desserts.

food.detail: This sour and spicy curry from Southern Thailand features fish, shrimp, and vegetables in a turmeric-based broth with distinctive salty and sour flavors.
Catch-of-the-day fish, prawns, and squid grilled whole over charcoal and served with spicy lime dipping sauce at beachfront restaurants.
A dry curry-style stir-fry with duck or chicken, featuring fragrant spices and typically served with crispy skin and fresh herbs.
Sweet sticky rice drizzled with coconut cream and topped with fresh ripe mango, a beloved Southern Thai dessert especially during mango season.
A light, aromatic broth with fresh fish, lemongrass, galangal, and chilies, often prepared with local catch at fishing villages and casual seafood spots.
Visit bustling night markets in Chaweng or Lamai where vendors serve affordable pad thai, satay skewers, mango salads, and other street favorites.

culture.detail: Never speak disrespectfully of the Thai monarchy and show reverence when their images appear on currency, in temples, or in public spaces.
Wear modest clothing (covered shoulders and knees) when visiting temples, remove shoes before entering sacred areas, and do not point feet at Buddha images.
Greet locals with a traditional wai (hands pressed together at chest with a slight bow), especially when thanking shopkeepers, guides, or service staff.
Expect to negotiate prices at local markets and small shops, but not in established restaurants or modern retail stores where prices are fixed.

places.name: Koh Tao
places.description: A small island renowned as one of the world's best and most affordable places to learn scuba diving. Koh Tao offers vibrant coral reefs, marine life, and a laid-back atmosphere popular with backpackers and divers.

activities.detail: Enroll in Open Water or Advanced certification courses at one of the island's many dive shops, with instruction at world-class coral sites and guaranteed small class sizes.
Explore a submerged rock formation teeming with barracuda, trevally, and occasional whale sharks during the dry season.
Wade into shallow, crystalline waters on the northwest coast to spot tropical fish, sea turtles, and vibrant coral without requiring certification.
Climb to the island's highest point for panoramic views of the Andaman Sea, nearby islands, and sunset over the water.
Descend to this dramatic dive site known for encounters with blacktip reef sharks, giant barracuda, and schooling fish.
Spend time on the island's longest and most popular beach, lined with beach bars, massage huts, and calm turquoise waters.
Take a speedboat to nearby islands like Koh Nangyuan or Koh Phangan for day trips featuring white sand beaches and additional dive sites.
Descend after sunset with guided instructors to witness nocturnal marine creatures, bioluminescence, and a completely transformed reef ecosystem.

food.detail: A fiery and aromatic soup made with fresh prawns, lemongrass, galangal, and lime juice, widely available at beachfront restaurants and local eateries.
Whole fish caught daily from local waters, grilled and served with chili dipping sauce, lime, and sticky rice at waterfront seafood shacks.
Stir-fried rice noodles with shrimp or chicken, bean sprouts, peanuts, and tamarind sauce; a staple comfort food served at street carts and restaurants island-wide.
A mild, rich curry blending meat, potatoes, peanuts, and warm spices, traditionally offered at Thai restaurants catering to international divers.

food.text: Fresh Coconut and Tropical Fruit Smoothies
food.detail: Blended drinks made from local mango, papaya, pineapple, and young coconut, sold at beachside juice stands and backpacker hangouts.

culture.detail: Follow dive shop guidelines strictly regarding coral protection, reef-safe sunscreen, and wildlife interaction, as the island's livelihood depends on preserving its delicate ecosystem.
Wear modest clothing when leaving the beach or entering temples; cover shoulders and knees as a sign of respect in Thai Buddhist culture.
Locals appreciate visitors who use simple greetings like 'Sawasdee krap' (hello) and 'Khob khun krap' (thank you); it fosters goodwill in this tight-knit island community.

culture.text: Avoid Negative Speech About the Monarchy
culture.detail: Thailand has strict laws protecting the royal family; refrain from any criticism or disrespect toward the king, queen, or their images in public spaces.

places.name: Phuket Old Town
places.nameIt: เมืองเก่าภูเก็ต
places.description: A charming historic area featuring colorful Sino-Portuguese architecture, vintage shops, and authentic local culture. Walking through the narrow streets reveals temples, museums, and cafes that showcase Phuket's rich heritage.

activities.detail: Walk the heart of Old Town along this tree-lined street lined with restored Sino-Portuguese shophouses painted in pastel yellows, blues, and pinks.
Learn about the tin mining era and Chinese immigration that shaped Phuket through photographs, artifacts, and exhibits housed in a restored mansion.

activities.text: Pay respects at Jui Tui Shrine
activities.detail: Observe this important Chinese temple with ornate decorations, especially vibrant during the annual Vegetarian Festival when devotees walk on hot coals.
Discover vintage clothing stores, antique shops, and local art galleries tucked into converted heritage buildings throughout the narrow streets.
Capture the well-preserved 19th-century buildings with their distinctive blue shutters, shuttered windows, and intricate Chinese temple details.
Explore this atmospheric Taoist temple with its iconic red lanterns and peaceful courtyard, a spiritual center for the local Chinese community.

food.detail: Silky egg noodles served in a rich, aromatic broth with vegetables and meat, a comfort food specialty found in Old Town's traditional noodle shops.
Crispy fried pastry filled with spiced meat and vegetables, a favorite street food of Phuket's Muslim community and Malay heritage.
Thick egg noodles and rice noodles combined with shrimp, squid, and pork in a savory soy-based sauce reflecting the town's Chinese-Thai fusion.
A tangy yellow curry with fish, vegetables, and tamarind that represents authentic Southern Thai cuisine found in family-run restaurants.
Enjoy grilled meat skewers with peanut sauce paired with traditional strong black coffee at a heritage shophouse cafe.

culture.detail: Old Town is most atmospheric before 10 AM when locals open their shops and the streets are less crowded, allowing authentic interaction with residents.
When entering shrines and temples, dress modestly with covered shoulders and knees, remove shoes at entrances, and observe quietly during worship.
The annual nine-day event transforms Old Town with parades, shrine processions, and special vegetarian food offerings—plan a visit if timing allows.
Understanding the Chinese merchant families who built these shophouses during the tin boom helps visitors appreciate the area's preserved architecture and cultural identity.

places.name: Khao Yai National Park
places.description: Thailand's largest and most visited national park, featuring lush jungles, cascading waterfalls, and abundant wildlife including elephants, gibbons, and hornbills. It's an essential destination for nature lovers and hikers seeking pristine rainforest experiences.

activities.detail: Hike to the park's most spectacular three-tiered waterfall where you can swim in emerald pools surrounded by rainforest canopy.
Join a guided nocturnal tour by vehicle or on foot to spot nocturnal wildlife including civets, flying squirrels, and owls active after dark.
Trek through dense forest on designated trails where wild Asian elephants frequently roam, especially near water sources during dry season.
Visit this lesser-known multi-level waterfall featuring a scenic pool perfect for swimming and picnicking away from crowded tourist areas.
Wake before dawn to trek the forest listening for white-handed gibbon calls echoing through the canopy and spot them swinging between trees.
Explore bamboo groves and open areas where great hornbills, pied hornbills, and other rare bird species can be observed year-round.

activities.text: Scenic Overlook at Khao Yai Mountain
activities.detail: Climb to elevated viewpoints within the park offering panoramic vistas across the jungle canopy and distant mountains on clear days.

food.detail: Spicy northeastern Thai minced meat salad traditionally made with locally-sourced game meats and served at park restaurants and nearby villages.

food.text: Som Tam with Fresh Bamboo Shoots
food.detail: Green papaya salad incorporating freshly harvested bamboo shoots from the forest, offering a crisp authentic flavor unique to the region.
Fresh freshwater fish caught from park ponds and grilled over charcoal, seasoned with lime, chili, and herbs at park lodges and restaurants.
Traditional northeastern meal of glutinous rice paired with seasonal foraged forest vegetables and jungle herbs prepared by local guides.
Northern Thai coconut curry noodle soup available at the visitor center and nearby restaurants, perfect for refueling after morning hikes.

culture.detail: Maintain at least 20 meters from elephants and other large animals, never attempt to feed or touch wildlife as they are wild and unpredictable.
Hiring an official park guide is mandatory for most trails and deeply appreciated as guides are community members who rely on tourism income.
Wildlife is most active and visible during early morning and late afternoon, so plan hikes accordingly and speak softly on trails to avoid disturbing animals.
Wear closed-toe hiking boots, long pants, and a hat to protect against leeches, insects, and rough terrain rather than casual tourist clothing.

places.name: Trang Beaches
places.nameIt: ชายหาดตรัง
places.description: Trang province offers pristine, less-crowded beaches and islands that remain relatively undeveloped compared to other southern destinations. It's ideal for travelers seeking authentic Thai beach culture and peaceful island exploration.

activities.detail: Explore vibrant coral reefs and tropical fish in the crystal-clear waters surrounding this pristine island, one of Trang's most biodiverse marine parks.

activities.text: Island-hop to Koh Lao and Koh Muk
activities.detail: Visit the dramatic emerald lagoon (Emerald Pool) inside Koh Muk's limestone cave accessible by kayak at high tide, then relax on white-sand beaches.
Paddle through dense mangrove forests to spot monitor lizards, sea eagles, and bioluminescent plankton on night kayaking tours.

activities.text: Walk Pak Meng Beach at sunset
activities.detail: Stroll the gentle 3-kilometer sandy beach on the mainland with local fishing boats returning, offering an authentic glimpse of daily coastal life.
Experience a slow-paced fishing community with no motorbikes, staying in bungalows and eating fresh seafood directly from local fishermen.
Descend to see Japan Airlines wreck, granite boulders, and abundant marine life in deeper waters favored by experienced divers.

food.detail: A tangy southern Thai curry made with turmeric, shallots, and fresh seafood, often served with local white fish and bitter herbs.
Grilled pork or chicken skewers brushed with turmeric paste and served with peanut sauce, a specialty found at beachside grills.

food.text: Khanom Jeen Nam Ya (fermented rice noodles with fish curry sauce)
food.detail: Soft rice noodles topped with a rich, slightly sweet fish-based curry sauce and fresh vegetables, eaten for breakfast or lunch.
Local seafood grilled over charcoal at beachfront restaurants and night markets, often seasoned simply with salt, lime, and chili.
A local variation of Thai roti filled with egg, sugar, or savory fillings and folded, popular as a snack from street vendors near beaches.

culture.detail: Early mornings and late afternoons see heavy boat traffic; avoid swimming near fishing zones and be mindful when taking photos of fishermen's daily routines.
While beach attire is casual, wear shirts and pants in village areas and temples out of respect for local Buddhist customs and conservative dress norms.
Trang sees fewer foreign tourists than popular southern destinations, so locals greatly appreciate attempts to speak Thai; a simple 'Sawasdee krap/ka' goes far.
Buddhist temples on islands like Koh Sukorn welcome respectful visitors, typically in morning hours; remove shoes and avoid pointing feet at Buddha images.

places.name: Koh Lanta
places.description: A laid-back island known for long sandy beaches, excellent snorkeling and diving, and a relaxed atmosphere that contrasts with busier destinations. Koh Lanta is perfect for diving enthusiasts and those seeking a quieter tropical escape.

activities.detail: A shallow, protected bay on the west coast ideal for beginners to explore coral gardens and tropical fish without strong currents.

activities.text: Diving at Rok and Haa Islands
activities.detail: Advanced dive sites south of Koh Lanta featuring dramatic underwater rock formations, vibrant coral walls, and encounters with large pelagic species.
The historic settlement on the island's east coast with traditional wooden stilt houses, local shops, and a glimpse into the island's seafaring heritage.
The island's longest and most popular beach stretching across the western shore, perfect for swimming, sunset watching, and beachfront dining.
Guided kayaking through dense mangrove forests on the island's southern tip to spot wildlife and learn about the local ecosystem.
A limestone freshwater lagoon surrounded by jungle cliffs offering swimming and scenic forest walks.

food.detail: A sour and spicy yellow curry made with turmeric and tamarind, typically prepared with local seafood and eaten with rice or roti.

food.text: Fresh Grilled Fish with Nam Pla
food.detail: Whole fish caught daily and grilled simply, served with lime-based fish sauce and fresh herbs, a staple at beachfront restaurants.
Tender squid skewered and grilled with a peanut-based satay sauce, often found at night markets and seaside stalls.
Sweet sticky rice topped with ripe local mango and coconut cream, a popular dessert available throughout the island.
Crispy fried flatbread served with sweet condensed milk and sugar, or savory versions with egg and meat from street vendors.

culture.detail: Cover shoulders and knees when visiting temples, remove shoes before entering sacred spaces, and avoid pointing at Buddhist images or monks.
The wai (hands pressed together gesture) is customary when greeting locals and showing respect; a simple 'Sawasdee krap/ka' goes a long way.
Shop at family-run guesthouses, eat at locally-owned restaurants, and hire local guides to ensure your money benefits the island community directly.
Koh Lanta operates on a slower pace than mainland Thailand; accept that some businesses may close during low season and schedules are flexible rather than rigid.

places.name: Nakhon Si Thammarat
places.nameIt: นครศรีธรรมราช
places.description: One of Thailand's oldest and most culturally significant cities, featuring the revered Wat Phra Mahathat temple and traditional shadow puppet theater. It offers insight into authentic southern Thai culture, craftsmanship, and spiritual heritage.
imageQueries: Wat Phra Mahathat Nakhon Si Thammarat

activities.detail: Explore Thailand's most revered temple complex, home to a 12th-century chedi said to contain Buddha relics and surrounded by intricate shrines and meditation halls.
Witness traditional Nang Talung shadow puppet theater, a centuries-old southern Thai art form performed with intricately carved leather puppets and live musical accompaniment.
Browse artifacts showcasing southern Thai history, royal regalia, and cultural heritage spanning from prehistoric times to the modern era.
Visit this serene temple known for its peaceful grounds and traditional Thai architecture, offering a quieter alternative to the main temple complex.
Stroll through the evening market near the city center to sample local snacks, crafts, and street food while mingling with locals.
Observe artisans practicing the ancient craft of niello inlay metalwork on silver, creating intricate patterns with stunning contrast.

food.detail: A tangy, spicy southern curry made with sour turmeric and fresh seafood or fish, distinctly sour and herbaceous with a fiery kick.
Grilled meat skewers served with thick, rich peanut sauce—a southern specialty often more generous with coconut and slightly sweeter than central Thai versions.
Soft rice noodles topped with a pungent, aromatic fish curry sauce made from freshwater fish, turmeric, and southern herbs.
A stuffed flatbread folded with savory fillings like minced meat, egg, and onions, then fried until crispy and served with sweet condensed milk or savory curry dip.
A mild, rich southern curry with chicken, peanuts, potatoes, and warm spices like cardamom and cinnamon, reflecting historical Malay-Muslim influence.

culture.detail: Remove shoes before entering temples, dress modestly with covered shoulders and knees, and never point your feet toward Buddha images or monks.
Nakhon Si Thammarat is less tourist-heavy than Bangkok; speaking a few Thai words and showing cultural interest will earn genuine smiles and warmer interactions.
The annual Songkran and Loy Krathong festivals draw devotees to Wat Phra Mahathat and fill streets with parades and traditional ceremonies reflecting deep local spirituality.
Purchasing nielloware, shadow puppets, or traditional crafts directly from workshops helps preserve centuries-old southern Thai craftsmanship and supports families.

places.name: Railay Beach
places.nameIt: หาดไร่เลย์
places.description: One of Thailand's most scenic beaches, accessible only by boat, surrounded by towering limestone cliffs and pristine sand. Railay is famous for world-class rock climbing, snorkeling, and its dramatic natural beauty.

activities.detail: Railay is world-renowned for sport climbing with hundreds of bolted routes on limestone cliffs ranging from beginner to advanced levels.
A freshwater emerald-colored lagoon nestled in the jungle, accessible via a short hike through dense vegetation and a natural cave passage.
Day trips by longtail boat to nearby islands like Bamboo Island and Phra Nang Cave Beach offer excellent snorkeling among coral reefs and tropical fish.
A stunning beach sheltered by limestone cliffs with a cave housing a shrine dedicated to a local princess, featuring golden stalactites and traditional phallic offerings.
A moderately challenging hike to a hilltop viewpoint offering panoramic views of East Railay, West Railay, and the surrounding limestone landscape.
Several beachfront schools offer hands-on classes teaching preparation of curry pastes, pad thai, and other Southern Thai specialties using local ingredients.
Guided kayak tours explore the mangrove channels and sea caves accessible from the beach, revealing limestone formations and local birdlife.
West Railay's western exposure provides stunning golden-hour views over the Andaman Sea, best experienced from beachfront bars and restaurants.

food.detail: Green papaya salad pounded with lime, fish sauce, and chilies is a ubiquitous and refreshing Southern Thai starter found at beachfront restaurants and street stalls.
Spicy and tangy shrimp soup infused with lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves, highlighting fresh seafood from local boats.
Grilled meat or seafood skewers served with rich coconut peanut sauce are popular beach snacks, often prepared fresh at evening food stalls.
Whole fish grilled over charcoal and seasoned with lime, garlic, and herbs takes advantage of daily catches from local fishermen and is served at waterfront restaurants.
A mild, rich curry with peanuts, potatoes, and meat reflects Muslim Malay influence in Southern Thailand and appears on most local menus.
Sweet sticky rice topped with fresh mango and condensed coconut cream is a beloved dessert best enjoyed during mango season from April to June.

culture.detail: When visiting Phra Nang Cave, wear respectful clothing and remove shoes before entering the shrine; avoid pointing at or disrespecting the religious offerings.
When arriving by longtail boat, be mindful of the boatman's instructions and avoid touching the sacred amulets and strings often hung on the vessel.
Railay's ecosystem is fragile; use reef-safe sunscreen, take all trash with you, and avoid stepping on coral during snorkeling trips.
The traditional Thai greeting of hands pressed together (wai) is appreciated by locals; use it when entering shops, restaurants, and when meeting guides.

restaurants.name: Krua Apsorn
restaurants.description: Legendary beachfront seafood shack serving ultra-fresh catches grilled to perfection with minimal fuss.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled fish with garlic and chili

restaurants.name: Blue Elephant
restaurants.description: Fine dining establishment celebrating authentic royal Thai cuisine in a restored colonial mansion.
restaurants.mustOrder: Gaeng Phed Ped Yang (red curry with duck)

restaurants.name: Bimi Beach Club
restaurants.description: Upscale beachfront venue blending Mediterranean and Southeast Asian flavours with stunning sunset views.
restaurants.mustOrder: Sea bass with lemongrass and coconut

restaurants.name: Khanom Jeen Nam Yaeng Nai Muan
restaurants.description: Simple, generations-old shophouse specializing in silky rice noodles with fragrant tamarind-based curry broth.
restaurants.mustOrder: Khanom Jeen Nam Yaeng (rice noodles with fish curry sauce)

restaurants.name: Savoey Seafood
restaurants.address: 1 Prachuap Khiri Khan Road, Hua Hin
restaurants.description: Casual, reliable seafood restaurant where fishermen's daily catches are transformed into simple, bold dishes.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled prawns with salt and pepper

restaurants.name: Chomtalay
restaurants.description: Beachside restaurant on Koh Samui delivering consistently excellent Thai seafood and comfort food.

restaurants.name: Peace Reggae Bar & Restaurant
restaurants.description: Laid-back beachfront spot with creative cocktails and a menu mixing Thai and international comfort cuisine.

restaurants.name: Ao Prao Resort Restaurant
restaurants.description: Intimate beachfront dining on a quieter island, serving fresh seafood prepared with traditional Thai techniques.

restaurants.name: Methavalai Sorndej
restaurants.description: No-frills local institution famous for its aromatic southern curries and authentic street food flavours.

restaurants.name: Kan Toke Dinner
restaurants.description: Traditional Thai experience with dancers and musicians, serving northern-style cuisine on low tables.

restaurants.name: Trang Province Seafood House
restaurants.description: Bustling local gem serving the freshest catches from Trang's fishing fleets, simply prepared and incredibly fresh.
restaurants.mustOrder: Steamed blue crab with chili paste

restaurants.name: Pla Pla Restaurant
restaurants.description: Contemporary dining celebrating the rich spice-forward flavours of southern Thailand's distinct regional cuisine.
restaurants.mustOrder: Kaeng Tai Pla (southern fish curry with bitter greens)

restaurants.name: Sunrise Restaurant
restaurants.description: Relaxed beachside eatery on quiet Koh Lanta serving honest Thai food with morning ocean views.

restaurants.name: Mom Tri's Kitchen
restaurants.description: Award-winning restaurant helmed by Thailand's first female celebrity chef, emphasizing heirloom recipes and refinement.
restaurants.mustOrder: Khao Soi Gai (northern-style curry noodles with chicken)
